title: #39 - The piano, the watch, and the farm

blocks.caption: Not so much biking - but a BIG day

blocks.text: On the third day in Italia - Italia gives me three amazing separate all crazy events...
Strap yourselves in, this is a longer one.

What a beautiful beautiful day. June 27th was my third day in Italy - and it felt like it went from zero to one hundred very fast. The interesting part about bike tours is that they are so unpredictable. June 27th is no different. I could have NEVER imaged this would happen, soon we will get to this.

I am writing this on the 28th, in the morning in a coffee shop. I have eaten some kind of cheese pie with mozzarella cheese, a cappuccino, Cornetto and espresso. Food is delicious in Italy! And the espresso is the best I have drunk. I am not sure how I am going to go back to regular coffee in Vancouver after this...
The traditional breakfast italiano is

items: Cornetto thats stuff with chocolate, crème, or jam. It's very tasty and is sweet. It goes great with the coffee.

blocks.text: Okay now into yet another unpredictable day. The journey to Matera.

Matera is a city that I have heard SO MUCH about. I met Italians in Greece... they say go to matera. I met Greeks in Greece... they say to to Matera. I meet Italians in Puglia... they say go to Matera.
So I had no choice but to plan it into the route as I cross the lower section of the Italian boot.
I have no idea WHY I need to go there, but I assumed that I would find out why everyone thinks it's a "must see".
I woke up on the beach where I camped, at 5 am. This is when the sun is starting to rise, I find it hard to keep sleeping when the sun starts to come out.

blocks.caption: Only one in the beach at 5 am

blocks.text: This was a massive beach, I could see for kilometres down one way, and giant windmills to my left beside the beach. In this moment, staring out at the ocean and the beach - was the first time that I actually felt alone. There was nobody. It wasn't bad, it wasn't good, I was just alone and it didn't bother me at all. It was just a passing thought I had.
"Oh shit, I am all alone here."
I packed up my stuff and took my time leaving the beach.
As I mentioned, the goal today was to get to Matera. So I looked on the map, a route to the first small town - I thought I might stop for lunch and avoid the heat.

I now have this golden rule. I will do anything to avoid busy highways. Yesterday when I accidentally took a highway it sucked big time. It's too stressful I cant relax and ride tranquilo. I don't care if I have to bike way steeper roads or trails, or longer distance. Quiet roads are the best way to go.
Thus I started biking on the quiet roads.
I biked through a small city called Palagiano. it was cool. Every town looks a little different I really like it.
After Palagiano I entered some farm roads, Only wide enough for one car going one direction at a time. No room for bikes on the side. The nice part was there were almost no cars.
I saw in the rear view mirror a car approaching. So I pulled over to the side to let it pass, and gave my usual friendly car wave.

I said "ciao" and continued biking behind the car. Then the car stopped maybe 10 metres ahead of me and he pulled over to the side. It looked like to let me pass. I biked by and he rolled down the window.
We started to chat and he was so surprised to see a biker on this road, we talked for a while. He had a farm, he had his fields to the side where I was biking and his house was up the road.
Then he told me to follow him behind his car, he was inviting me for something but I only understand like 85% of what people say here lol and I missed what I was being invited for. I went anyway...
He pulled over at one point and grabbed two watermelons from his field.
"Aww great he wanted me to try fruit". I thought.
Then we continued, eventually we got to his farm house. it was stunning. I really liked the colours. It reminded me of the building I really liked the day before in Taranto.
Then I met his wife, she came out from the house to say hi. We all talked for a while, they brought out fruit for me to eat, all produced from their fields.
His wife noticed that I had a plastic disposable water bottle on my bike and only one reusable one. I mentioned I lost my water bottle in Lecce.
Then they gave me a new water bottle! Wow. I was very grateful.

We sat and ate fruit, he cut the watermelon with a knife and asked me if I had a knife. I said no - then he gave me the knife too! Again I was very grateful. Then their other friend came up to the house. We were all talking and she had a Pizzaria in the city I biked from, unfortunately it was closed that day, but she gave me a recommendation for where to eat dinner in Matera the next day. She knew everything about pizza. Slowly I am learning :).

blocks.caption: The fresh fruit and water bottle

blocks.text: They left since they had to run errands and I sat there alone for a while eating the fruit in the shade. When I finished I left a little thank you note saying if they came to Vancouver, I would be their guide.
I got the instagram of Raffillia, who as the friend who owned the pizzeria - and then all the others through her.

The original man I met left a really nice comment no my last instagram post in Italian. That made me happy :). Wow Italians are such nice people. They love to chat with anyone, and seem to really enjoy helping people. (If you guys read this thank you again. Una cosa bella che abbiate fatto per me ❤️)
I kept biking, feeling fresh from the fruit and water.

Amazing quite farm roads and gravel. I called my friend Colton and we talked for a while while I biked with the trails. We hadn't talked for a while and it was nice. Let's see if he reads this haha. (A little Easter egg)
At one point I crossed this bridge just for bikers and it had a nice view. I love when I stumble upon on a road that is made for bikers :)
I also love the fact that I can read the signs again. In Greece I could read them, but instantly forget them since I wasn't able to remember Greek names at all.

This took me to the next town called Castellaneta. I got to the town around 11:30 and I was tempted to bike to one more town and skip this one. Mainly since I had another 35 km to Matera and I wanted to get as far as possible before stopping for the afternoon.
But I learned from yesterdays mistakes. I did NOT want to miss lunch again having everything closed at 2 or 3 pm. So I stopped.
I biked up a hill and completely at random stopped to ask an Italian guy if he knew a good Trattoria to eat at. At this point i still had not been able to find an open one to eat at!
I was happy to hear that he knew a place. AND he said I could follow him in his car, me with the bike behind.
So I did. He took me down the hill I had just painfully climbed to a Trattaria he said was the best one in the city!
Of course it was closed.... Damnit. Turns out Tuesday is a day of closure for this place.
But it did not end there. We chatted for a while and he had another recommendation farther away and he. showed me on the map how to get there.

We talked more and eventually he said he was going to cook lunch for his wife and her friend at his apartment just where I met him getting into the car. His wife was from Korea. She did not speak Italian, and he did not Korean, but they communicated in English. I thought this was very cool!
Next he invited me for coffee with all of them after I finished eating lunch in the afternoon.
I accepted and went to the food place he recommended to eat.
I got there and it was ALMOST closed. The coffee part was open but lunch wasn't open for another hour and a half. Is anything every open in Italy?

I sat and got coffee, then as I was sitting there for a while an older Italian man and I started talking. He joined me at my table and we talked for a long time about his life as a singer travelling around Italy.
At the end of our conversation when he had to go and I was going to start eating lunch, I noticed he had a nice watch.
"I like your watch, it's nice", I said something like this.
"You like it?" He replied.
Then he took the watch off and put it on my wrist.
He said. "It's yours now"
He just gave me his watch! I was so shocked yet again. I refused to take it first but he WOULD NOT take it back.
He was very adamant that I take it.

Well we parted ways and I ate some nice pasta in the restaurant. It was good - It did not blow me away, I need to try more pasta here to compare. I thought it was a little undercooked (Italians who read this please don't get offended). I like pasta Al Dante but this was a little TOO Al Dante.
The plate looked pretty.
I left the restaurant and I went to the house of Antonio, the man who had given me the recommendations for where to eat.
I got there and I went into the apartment, the first thing I see is a massive grand piano in the apartment.
I met his wife and her friends. They were two more Koreans living in Italy, friends of Antonio's Wife.
They invited me to sit and we chatted, they gave me beer, pizza, and tiramisu.
Turns out Antonino is not just a regular guy.
He is a professional Piano player.
We gathered around the piano and Antonio played. He didnt just play. he PLAYED. Oh my God I have never seen someone play like that.
He was played songs that he composed. I was completely in shock. I guess this is how a professional pianist plays - it's a whole other level.

If you want to hear how he plays. Here is a link to his Spotify, where he had songs he has composed. Check them out! If you like them add them to liked songs since that helps other people find his music.
He played "The Rain" for us live and it was incredible.
The Rain
Antonio Di Fonzo · Song · 2019
open.spotify.com/track/2bhmbAKMplEbNjLYTZAo1d?si=PZDiMtF0RjqGTtliWuD2Jg
This song above is one he played lived in his apartment for us.
Later we played some classic hits and I sang as he played it was very fun.
Another coincidence...
The two Korean friends live in Matera! They invited me to go with them in their car so I did not have to bike the whole way in the heat.
I said no thank you. I explained that I love biking and I like the challenge. It looked like a fun ride.

Then I drank with them, Antonio's wife was extremely kind to me and kept pouring me more and more beer, eventually came the brandy, then the wine. THE WINE. It was red wine and red wine made by Antonio's father. It was some of the best red I ever had as well.
After this point it was already 5 or 6 PM and let's say I was a little tipsy.
I reconsidered the ride offer to Matera...
I asked if it was still on the table and they said yes.
Eventually we left and said goodbye and I put my bike in the back of the car. We drove to Matera together. Cars are way faster.

Hold up. This is a bike tour. Am I cheating? I thought so at first, then I said, no this is fine. Im just having fun. I don't need to be so strict with always biking. It would have been more dangerous to bike in the dark hammered. I was happy with the decision. I am still having to bike another 6000 kilometres or so, I'll cut myself some slack.

I still had no idea where I was going to sleep. I think they felt bad for me and Yunmi (On of the Korean friends) offered me a spot in their home. I said even outside would be fine since i had the tent. I went to her home and met her husband. It was fun trying to explain why I was in her car with my bike in Italian haha, everything's always in Italian in Italy. It's obvious but I love it. But he was very kind as well and super helpful, we had beer and Yunmi cooked dinner for everyone.
They had a little multi-poodle dog that reminded me a lot of Shamrock, my dog who passed away last year. Just like Shamrock, their dog would not stop barking at me hahaha.

After dinner, Giovanni (Yunmi's husband) had an amazing idea. He told me everything I need to do the next day in the city since we was born and raised there. And he told me that I was welcome to camp in their home but if I did that it would be hard to get to Matera the next day (Since they lived in the outskirts of the city) and he said the view from this spot was incredible.
After dinner we drove together to the spot he said was a great view.
WOW.
Here were the nights accommodations
What a day.
-Erik
